text: Hello everyone and welcome to another episode of This is My Model. If you're a traditional
viewer you'll know that we always host customers and talk about their internal architectures and
platforms. However, today we're doing something a little bit different. We're turning the cameras
on ourselves internally to learn a little bit more about how the AWS DeepRacer service actually works.
I'm joined by Dr. Shahika Gensh from the Amazon SageMaker RL team. Shahika, thanks for joining us.
Thanks for having me. There's so many different types of techniques out there when it comes to
machine learning. Specifically, there's supervised learning and unsupervised learning. Why are we
using reinforcement learning? Reinforcement learning has a very high barrier to entry. There are a lot
of skill set that is needed and to be acquired in order to learn how you can use reinforcement learning
to solve complex artificial intelligence problems. So let's jump right into it, put a marker to the
board and just figure out from an architecture perspective where we start and what services
make up the AWS DeepRacer service. There are five main services that make up the SageMaker,
RoboMaker, S3, Kinesis and CloudWatch. DeepRacer in the back runs all these services.
So this is the console view from the customer's perspective after they log in and they're
interacting right away with the core services within their VPC.
That is true. Both Amazon SageMaker and AWS RoboMaker are under AVPC.
So Amazon SageMaker for training, AWS RoboMaker for the simulation environment where I'm assuming the
agent is exploring, understanding and learning over time given its environment that it's working in.
That is true. Amazon SageMaker has several training algorithms using popular reinforcement learning
algorithms. AWS RoboMaker creates a simulation environment where your physics engine determines
given a sensor input and an action how your car is going to behave. So the physics engine is already
pre-configured, the environment is already pre-created and the parameters from a configuration perspective
are already pre-configured for the users. That is already done and it is done specifically for DeepRacer.
That will allow your car to run in the real world as well. So a lot of the interaction
happens between Amazon SageMaker and AWS RoboMaker. We'll discuss more about that through the neural
network discussion, but let's talk about the other services that make up the AWS DeepRacer service.
That's a great question. Amazon SageMaker uses S3 to store information about your presets,
which is your hardware parameters, your model, and in addition to that it stores information about your
environment. So one of the important things when it comes to reinforcement learning is the reward function,
right? So that logic of how to incentivize an agent within its environment. When I author that,
what happens to that logic? That logic stores in the S3 bucket and what happens is that the model and all the logic
later is used by AWS RoboMaker to generate new experiences that the Amazon SageMaker can learn from.
As RoboMaker generates new experiences, state, next state, action, and reward,
Amazon SageMaker interacts through the S3 bucket to obtain that information and to get better and learn
to drive the car. Very cool. So if I do something creative within my reward function, I'll have the
confidence that it's being stored for later viewing. Kinesis video streams, I'm thinking a stream from the
simulation environment or a first person view in the console. Can you talk to us about that?
That is true. You want to see how the car is driving and Kinesis video stream allows you to have that
windshield view that the car is seeing while it is trying to learn by trial and error. So it's very cool
because I can actually see it going off the track right away during the exploration phase and as it
exploits its knowledge and its learnings over time, it's hopefully making a lap around the track.
That is true and it may get very addicted at some point.
Now from a logging perspective, I'm trying to put my developer hat on. I want to make sure that I can
view all the different logs, print out all the parameters from my reward function, but talk to me
about what are we logging specifically with Amazon CloudWatch? With Amazon CloudWatch, we are tracking
several information. We want to learn when the algorithm is exploiting and when the algorithm is
exploring. Exploring phase is when the algorithm is trying to learn many different new behaviors.
Exploiting is when it is trying to maximize that behavior. We store information from both RoboMaker and
SageMaker in order to be able to plot the reward function and also the progress the car makes along
with how the exploration is going on. So let's jump right into the Manage Jupiter Notebook and Amazon
SageMaker and really understand the neural network architecture, how it's created. Can you help us
walk through what to expect from the initial image that the agent sees until the output? Great. Our Amazon
SageMaker reinforcement learning for DeepRacer is Proximate Policy Optimization. It is one of the
state-of-art algorithms. This algorithm works by creating an agent model as well as a critic model,
which is representative of your environment. So the neural network we're going to discuss is going to be
the agent model that inputs the image as its information and it outputs actions. Hence, by updating the weights,
the agent learns how to drive. Okay, great. So it all starts with a 160 by 120 image that's passed over to a
convolutional neural network. That is true. So let's have our actions at the very end as the output and we
have our input as the image. And we have several convolutional neural networks that learns to focus
on several points. It may be focusing on yellow lines or it may be focusing on white lines depending
your reward function. We pass this image through three layers of convolutional neural networks where
the car learns to extract several features that will enable it to learn to drive. So the final stage
before we reach to the action states after our convolutional neural networks is the fully connected
layers and these layers enable which actions to be taken. So this is where the policy
network is created. This is where PPO really comes into play and the weights are constantly updated for
the retrainings that happen. That is true. At every training, one SageMaker, Amazon SageMaker is running
with the information that it receives from RoboMaker, it will be making updates to these weights.
So this is open sourced right now. Anybody can go out, look at the actual AWS samples, Amazon SageMaker RL
repository, look at the AWS DeepRacer example and even begin to modify it a little bit. What are some of
the modifications that you've seen that have worked out very well for customers? Some of the modifications
includes changing this architecture and this architecture can be changed in one of the files
that you are storing in S3 bucket. So if you forget about what you did, you can still go back and look at
what your architecture is. It also stores information about your hyperparameters.
Okay, great. So that preset file, the hyperparameters, I can change those, maybe even tune those,
add different types of layers. So that's that modularity flexibility that we give you with an
open source notebook. And really that kind of addresses one aspect of the reality gap. Can you talk to us
more about that big challenge and issue within the industry when it comes to the reality gap and
how we can address that using the Amazon SageMaker notebook? It's been very difficult to bridge the
gap between simulation and reality. In order to reduce that gap, what we did behind the scenes is we
made sure that the simulation has high physics fidelity. That is, the way the car behaves in physics is the
same as the car will behave in simulation. And furthermore, through extensive testing, we learned that
there are several network models that will generalize much better into the real world. And
current default architecture for DeepRacer is one of those networks. But we found through research that
there are several of those that could be utilized. Great. So it sounds like there's much more to come
with AWS DeepRacer as well. There's constant updates that are happening to the notebook end. There's
flexibility of choice around how to get started, whether you have little to no knowledge of machine
learning or whether you're an expert or a practitioner, as you put it. And at the end of the day, upskilling
your workforce and just learning about reinforcement learning in general has now been lowered from a
barrier perspective. And this is really true to marketization of reinforcement learning. That is true.
And in addition to DeepRacer, Amazon SageMaker reinforcement learning repo contains many other notebooks
where you can bring your own simulation and or you can bring your own popular reinforcement learning
algorithms. Great. Shahika, thank you so much. It's always a pleasure. Thank you. And thank you for watching. This is my model.
